title: Shark Teeth in Puppies: A Guide to Retained Deciduous Teeth
meta_desc: Learn about retained baby teeth in dogs, why a 'double row' occurs, and why you should schedule a vet visit for your puppy's dental health.
content_html:
What are Retained Baby Teeth?

As puppies grow, their deciduous (baby) teeth should naturally fall out to make room for permanent adult teeth. When a baby tooth fails to fall out while the adult tooth erupts, it results in a "double row" of teeth, often nicknamed shark teeth. This most commonly occurs with the canine teeth (the long fangs) and incisors.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for retained baby teeth is generally Low. While it is not a life-threatening medical emergency, it does require professional attention. If left untreated, these extra teeth can cause overcrowding, painful malocclusions (bad bites), premature periodontal disease, and permanent damage to the adult teeth. You should schedule a non-emergency appointment with your veterinarian to discuss extraction, which is often performed during a spay or neuter procedure.

How a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear photo of your dog's mouth allows a veterinary professional to assess the degree of crowding and look for signs of gum inflammation or infection. Seeing the placement of the "double row" helps us determine how quickly the teeth need to be addressed and if there are immediate concerns regarding your puppy's comfort or bite alignment.

faqs.question: When should puppy baby teeth normally fall out?
faqs.answer: Most puppies lose their deciduous teeth and have a full set of adult teeth by 6 to 7 months of age.

faqs.question: Will the baby teeth fall out on their own if the adult teeth are already in?
faqs.answer: If the adult tooth has fully erupted and the baby tooth is still firmly in place, it is unlikely to fall out on its own and will typically require surgical extraction by a veterinarian.

faqs.question: Can I pull the retained tooth at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to pull a dog's tooth. Puppy teeth have surprisingly long, fragile roots that can break easily, leading to significant pain and potential infection if the root is left behind.
